name: MacTutor
biography:
Georg Faber studied mathematics and physics at the universities of Munich and G횄쨋ttingen between 1896 and 1901 . In 1902 he received a doctorate from the Ludwig-Maximilians University in Munich for a thesis 횄혵ber Reihenentwicklungen analytischer Funktionen 창혫혟 ( On series expansions of analytic functions ) . He received his university teaching qualification from the University of W횄쩌rzburg in 1905 , after submitting his Habilitationsschrift presenting work on power series in several variables.
He worked at a number of universities, being appointed to the University of T횄쩌bingen as an extraordinary professor in 1909 , then a year later as an ordinary professor at the Technische Hochschule in Stuttgart.  He remained in Stuttgart until 1912 when he moved to the University of K횄쨋nigsberg for a year, again moving on, this time to the University of Strasbourg. He was appointed to the chair of Higher Mathematics at the Technische Hochschule in Munich in 1916 . This was a post he held until he retired in 1946 .
Faber's most important work was on the polynomial expansion of functions. This is the problem of expanding an analytical function in an area bounded by a smooth curve as a sum of polynomials, where the polynomials are determined by the area.  These polynomials are now known as 'Faber polynomials' and first appear in Faber's 1903 paper 횄혵ber polynomische Entwickelungen 창혫혟 ( On polynomial developments ) published in Mathematische Annalen . Another important paper which he also published in Mathematische Annalen , this time in 1909 , was 횄혵ber stetige Funktionen 창혫혟 ( On continuous functions ) . In this paper he introduced the 'hierarchical basis' and explicitly used it for the representation of functions. In fact Faber was building on the idea of Archimedes who computed approximately using a hierarchy of polygonal approximations of a circle. Only in the 1980 s was Faber's idea seen to be an important ingredient for the efficient solution of partial differential equations.
Most of Faber's publications are in function theory. For example in addition to the papers mentioned above his publications include: 횄혵ber die Fortsetzbarkeit gewisser Taylorscher Reihen 창혫혟 ( On thehe continuability of certain Taylor's series ) (1903) ; 횄혵ber arithmetische Eigenschaften analytischer Funktionen 창혫혟 ( On arithmetic properties of analytic functions ) (1904) ; 횄혵ber die Abz횄짚hlbarkeit der rationalen Zahlen 창혫혟 ( On the countability of the rational numbers ) (1905) ; 횄혵ber analytische Funktionen mit vorgeschriebenen Singularit횄짚ten 창혫혟 ( On analytic functions with prescribed singularities ) (1905) ; 횄혵ber die zusammengeh횄쨋rigen Konvergenzradien von Potenzreihen mehrerer Ver횄짚nderlicher 창혫혟 ( On matching radii of convergence of power series of several variables ) (1905) ; 횄혵ber das Anwachsen analytischer Funktionen 창혫혟 ( On the growth of analytic functions ) (1907) ; Beitrag zur Theorie der ganzen Funktionen 창혫혟 ( Contribution to the theory of entire functions ) (1910) ; and 횄혵ber die interpolatorische Darstellung stetiger Funktionen 창혫혟 ( On the interpolating representation of continuous functions ) (1914) . However, he also edited the collected works of Christoffel and volumes 14 , 15 , and 16 of Euler 's collected works.
One further achievement of Faber is worthy of mention. In 1894 Lord Rayleigh made the following claim:- ... given a fixed area of ox-hide to make a drum, the ground tone is lowest if you make your drum circular. Two mathematicians independently verified Rayleigh 's conjecture, Faber and Edgar Krahn.
Faber was also interested in mathematical education and he worked with his colleague von Dyck at Munich on the mathematical education of engineers, physicists and mathematicians. In addition to his research areas, Faber lectured on complex analysis, probability theory , the theory of relativity and analytical mechanics.
When World War II ended in 1945 , Faber was appointed rector of the Technische Hochschule in Munich by the government. He organised restarting teaching in the university, which happened in the spring of 1946 , before retiring later that year. His achievements were marked by his election to the Bavarian Academy of Sciences in 1921 , the award of the Order of Merit in 1956 , and he received the Bavarian Service medal in 1959 .
Faber had many interests outside mathematics. He was a great linguist enjoying the study of both classical and modern languages, he loved music and art, and he enjoyed long walks.
